content.full_text:
August 15, 1977


Mr. Jay Patterson
Chief Deputy Registrar
Office of Registrar of Voters
City Hall
San Francisco, California  94102

Dear Mr. Patterson:

Regina is on vacation; so your letter of July 28, 1977 has
been referred to me for reply.

Our staff concurs with your advice to Mr. Ricardo Alfaro
concerning professional services donated by an individual
to a campaign.  There is no distinction between voluntary
personal services and services voluntarily rendered by a
professional.  Such services are not reportable as contri-
butions under the Political Reform Act.

I hope this information is helpful.  If our staff may offer
further assistance, please contact us.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        NAN EMMERSON
                                        Special Compliance Representative

NE:mak

bcc:  Jeanne P.
      Nan-LA Office

RM 63

CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO

OFFICE OF
REGISTRAR OF VOTERS
CITY HALL
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94102

July 28, 1977

Ms Regina Siciliano-Kutchins
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
926 J Street
Sacramento, California

Dear Regina:

I would like to get your official or unofficial opinion
on this.

The question raised concerns the local $500 Contribution
limitation, which is not within FPPC jurisdiction, but a
related issue, whether or not volunteer professional
services are contributions within the meaning of 82015 G.C.
(last paragraph) and whether they need be reported.

If the voluntary services of a lawyer are reportable where
does one draw the line? There is not much difference that
I can see between a professional lawyer who functions as a
campaign's delivery man or a professional clerk who serves
the campaign in that capacity.

Yours truly,

Jay Patterson
Chief Deputy Registrar

JP:m
Encls.

/ FORM 63

CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO

OFFICE OF                                    July 23, 1977
REGISTRAR OF VOTERS
CITY HALL
SAN FRANCISCO 2, CALIFORNIA


Mr. Ricardo J. Alfaro II, Treasurer
Denning for Super visor Committee
1812 Webster Street
San Francisco, California 94115

Dear Mr. Alfaro:

Your letter raises an interesting question concerning the
valuation of professional service in an election campaign.

My opinion is that services volunteered by a lawyer or
graphic artist would be exempt from the reporting require-
ments and contribution limitations, just as any other
voluntary services would be. This exemption does not apply
to any materials or supplies used by the volunteer in
performing his work, nor does it extend to paid workers in
the employ of the volunteer (for example, the lawyer's
secretary) unless they, too, are working on their own time.

This answer pre-supposes that the professional who is
volunteering his or her services is either self-employed or
working on his or her own time.

I am forwarding your inquiry to the Fair Political Practices
Commission but, because of the back log, a timely answer is
not expected. Until you receive further word, you may value
donated professional services in the same way as other services,
subject to the three conditions described here.

                                        Yours very truly,

                                        THOMAS P. KEARNEY
                                        Registrar of Voters-Recorder


                                        Jay Patterson
                                        Chief Deputy Registrar-Recorder

JP:m

    P.S.  If you have a lawyer/volunteer, why
          don't you ask him?


                                        COPY

Corporate Communications

1812 Webster Street
San Francisco
California 94115
415 567-3862 Office
415 563-4769 Residence


July 19, 1977


Registrar of Voters
City & County of San Francisco,
Room 155,
City Hall,
San Francisco, California 94102

Dear Sir:

In behalf of the Denning For Supervisor Committee, I request
clarification and, perhaps, judgement of a question relating to a
possible limitation to campaign contributions.

Section 16.508 of the San Francisco Administrative Code (under
Article XII), paragraph (a), limits "any contribution which will cause
the total amount contributed" by a person in behalf of a candidate in
an election to $500. Does this limit pertain only to money (eg. dollars)?

Let me pose a situation which will make it more easy to understand. The "campaign statement for committees" (Form 420) has a schedule
C which calls for the reporting of non-monetary contributions. Among
these are goods and services contributed to the committee or candidate.
It is our understanding that professional services donated to the campaign are to be reported - at their fair market value - in this schedule.
The question is as follows: Can we accept professional services of,
say, a graphic designer or an attorney whose "fair market value" may exceed the $500 limitation? I should point out that I am speaking of donated professional services, not voluntary personal services. For example, if an attorney spends time looking into various legal matters in
behalf of the campaign, and does not charge us for his time, these are
voluntary professional services. On the other hand, should the same person assist us with a mailing, or in telephone bank operation, I would
consider these volunteered personal services.

I would appreciate hearing from you at your earliest opportunity
because this matter could easily become a reality, and we, obviously,
do not wish to violate the rules.

Thank you, in advance, for your attention to this request.

Very truly yours,

Ricardo J. Alfaro II
Treasurer,
Denning for Supervisor Committee
(California Political I.D. No. 770939)

sections.question: Whether volunteer professional services are reportable as contributions under the Political Reform Act, and whether there is a distinction between voluntary personal services and services voluntarily rendered by a professional.
sections.conclusion: Such services are not reportable as contributions under the Political Reform Act. There is no distinction between voluntary personal services and services voluntarily rendered by a professional.
sections.question_synthetic: Are volunteer professional services (such as those provided by lawyers or graphic designers) reportable contributions under the Political Reform Act, or are they treated the same as voluntary personal services?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that volunteer professional services are not reportable as contributions under the Political Reform Act, and that there is no legal distinction between voluntary personal services and services voluntarily rendered by professionals.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes incoming request from Ricardo Alfaro and follow-up inquiry from Jay Patterson (Registrar of Voters). FPPC response written by Nan Emmerson, Special Compliance Representative. The document demonstrates the chain of inquiry and FPPC's definitive stance on volunteer professional services.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether volunteer professional services are reportable as contributions under the Political Reform Act, and whether there is a distinction between voluntary personal services and services voluntarily rendered by a professional.

CONCLUSION: Such services are not reportable as contributions under the Political Reform Act. There is no distinction between voluntary personal services and services voluntarily rendered by a professional.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to an inquiry regarding whether volunteer professional services donated to a campaign must be reported as contributions under the Political Reform Act. The FPPC advises that such services are not reportable contributions and are treated the same as other voluntary personal services.
